item.title: Spain: Control, composure, champions
item.description:
Four years ago, Argentina rode out countless storms en route to the title, eventually defeating France 4-2 on penalties after a breathtaking 3-3 draw in the final. This year’s showpiece in New York New Jersey saw La Albiceleste cede the throne to Spain, who claimed the crown by imposing order and maintaining constant control.
Spain capped their North American campaign with a display of technical and tactical excellence in the final, shutting down the opposition attack more effectively than ever. After stifling France and their fearsome forwards in a 2-0 semi-final win, the European champions turned their attention to Lionel Messi & Co, who became the latest victims of La Roja’s relentless march.
The Argentines had scored at least two goals in each of their previous matches, but this time they were uncharacteristically quiet. Lionel Scaloni’s men did not muster a single shot on target in 120 minutes, their only attempts coming in extra time and never truly troubling Unai Simon in the Spain goal.
“We’ve got a wonderful team,” said right-back Pedro Porro, speaking exclusively with FIFA. “We put up a great fight, right from the first minute. We’ve done that from the start against Cabo Verde, when we kept possession and created chances, but they just didn’t go in.”
In a match where the stakes could not have been any higher, Spain never lost their way or their focus. They controlled the tempo without ever losing their composure, just as they had in their 1-0 win over Portugal in the last 16 and a 2-1 triumph over Belgium in the quarters, when the winning goal was slow to materialise. Aside from their technical dominance, the Spanish stayed remarkably calm at a time when doubt could easily have set in.
“We stayed true to our style, I think that’s obvious,” continued Porro.
Luis de la Fuente's side had laid the foundations for their North American campaign at EURO 2024 in Germany, but this time round their style of play also relied on lightning-fast counter-attacks with jet-heeled wingers Lamine Yamal and Nico Williams. They were perhaps more direct two years ago and were more willing to engage in open matches, conceding four goals in their four knockout matches.
In North America, however, they remarkably shipped just a solitary goal, scored by Belgium’s Charles De Ketelaere, an unprecedented feat in the history of the tournament.
What has certainly changed over the past two years is the influence of the team’s metronome Rodri, winner of the adidas Golden Ball for the tournament’s best player. Holding the FIFA World Cup™ Trophy in his arms, the midfield maestro explained to FIFA how Spain were able to dominate all of their opponents.
“I think we’re the team that handles the most game situations,” he said. “Pressing, possession, counter-attacks, set pieces… I think that, in every aspect of football, Spain are the team that can control the game the best.”
When Rodri steps up the pace, so do Spain. When he chooses to disrupt the opposition’s rhythm, the whole team follows suit with remarkable composure, without ever losing their shape. La Roja’s 2026 vintage is not content to simply dominate possession, however, as they also dictate what their opponents do when they have the ball.
The newly crowned world champions were perhaps slightly less spectacular than they were two years ago, but this time they made control their greatest strength.
“We knew we wouldn’t conceded a lot of goals,” admitted Porro. “But we also knew that we had what it took to compete with anyone. In the end, we were rewarded with a wonderful prize.”
Four years ago, it was Argentina who emerged victorious from a storm in Qatar. This year, it was Spain who lifted the trophy by bringing a sense of calm back to proceedings. In a tournament that always seemed destined to be dominated by the teams with the strongest attacks, it was ultimately La Roja who carved out a path to the very top of the global game.
Courtesy: FIFA

Measles outbreak: Death toll reaches 793 as 5 more die in 24 hours
item.description:
Five more children died with symptoms consistent with measles in the 24 hours to 8am Monday, bringing Bangladesh's combined tally of confirmed and suspected measles-related deaths to 793.
The Directorate General of Health Services (DGHS) classified the latest deaths as suspected measles fatalities.
With the update, the number of suspected measles deaths has risen to 698, while the number of laboratory-confirmed measles deaths remained unchanged at 95.
A total of 922 new suspected measles cases were reported during the period, bringing the cumulative number of suspected cases nationwide to 118,570.
Meanwhile, 147 new confirmed measles cases were recorded, bringing the total number of laboratory-confirmed infections to 14,578.
Since March 15, a total of 101,026 patients with suspected measles have been hospitalized across the country, of whom 97,532 have recovered, according to DGHS data.

item.title: PM orders transparent rollout of Family Card program
Shama Obaed: Dhaka writes to Delhi seeking ex-home minister's extradition
item.description:
State Minister for Foreign Affairs Shama Obaed Islam on Monday said Bangladesh has sought the extradition of former Home Minister Asaduzzaman Khan Kamal, noting that a letter was sent to India through diplomatic channels seeking his extradition.
"Since he is a fugitive, we have given the letter that is required for his return. It has been sent recently, addressing only the former Home Minister," she told reporters while responding to a question after attending a seminar in the city.
The state minister said it is an ongoing process and people wish to see the court orders implemented against those who have been convicted and are fugitives including former prime minister Sheikh Hasina.
She said the government wants a fair trial, and feedback on Dhaka's extradition request depends on India.
"We will wait. We expect India will reply and will play its role in strengthening the relations between the two countries," Shama Obaed said.
Responding to a question, she said normally extradition request letters are sent separately and that they are sending letters for all individuals who remain fugitives.
The state minister on Thursday said former Prime Minister Sheikh Hasina, currently staying in India, would be arrested whenever she comes to Bangladesh.
"Since she has been convicted, there is no scope for surrender. Whenever she comes to Bangladesh, she will be arrested. It will be done in accordance with the law," she told reporters at the Ministry of Foreign Affairs, noting that efforts are continuing to secure her extradition to Bangladesh.
The state minister said the people of Bangladesh want court orders to be implemented against those living abroad. She said the Law Adviser and the Home Adviser had already explained the legal aspects.
"If she surrenders, the existing law will be followed. She will have to go to jail. The law will take its own course," Shama Obaed told reporters on Monday at the Ministry of Foreign Affairs.
She said Hasina's recent remarks from abroad appeared intended to encourage Awami League leaders and activists who have either gone into hiding or fled the country. "I see no other reason," she said.
Bangladesh has formally requested India to extradite Hasina under the bilateral extradition treaty.
Bangladesh is making sustained diplomatic efforts to bring Hasina back to face trial while acknowledging that the extradition process takes time.
Three ministries, including the Ministry of Foreign Affairs, have been pursuing the matter since the new government assumed office in February, building on efforts initiated under the interim government.
The government believes the people of Bangladesh want Hasina to be extradited.
On April 8, Bangladesh renewed its request for the extradition of Hasina and former Home Minister Asaduzzaman Khan Kamal, both of whom have been sentenced to death by the International Crimes Tribunal.
"We will continue to seek her extradition through a transparent judicial process. It has been discussed and will be dealt with through a transparent judicial process," Prime Minister's Foreign Affairs Adviser Humaiun Kobir told reporters in Dhaka.

item.title: 3 sentenced to death over separate rape-murders
item.description:
Separate courts in Magura, Chuadanga, and Chittagong on Monday sentenced three men to death in three separate cases involving the rape and murder of two girls and the murder of a child following an attempted rape.
A district court sentenced a man to death for raping and killing a teenage girl in 2022.
Magura District and Sessions Judge Mizanur Rahman delivered the verdict against Hasan Sheikh, 28, son of Fazlu Sheikh of Srikol village in Sreepur upazila.
According to the prosecution, the girl, a class VI student, went missing after leaving her home on March 17, 2022. She was found dead near a bamboo grove along the Kumar River the same day.
The girl's father filed a case at Sreepur Police Station. Police arrested Hasan Sheikh from the village on March 19 that year.
A tribunal sentenced the lone accused to death for the rape and murder of a 10-year-old girl in Jibannagar upazila in 2023.
Judge Md Moktagir Alam of the Women and Children Repression Prevention Tribunal delivered the verdict and fined the convict, Abul Hossain alias Fatik, 29, son of Abu Taher Molla of Bara Masjidpara in Goyeshpur village, Tk200,000. He was present in the dock for the judgment.
According to the prosecution, at around 11am on December 21, 2023, the 10-year-old daughter of van driver Iqbal Hossain, went to collect vegetables in a field with several other children. Abul Hossain lured her into a nearby maize field on the pretext of helping her, then asked the other children to wait.
When she did not return, the children informed their mother. Her family later found her body in the maize field with her throat slit.
The girl's father filed a murder case at Jibannagar Police Station against unidentified persons. Sub-Inspector Saiful Islam, the investigating officer, submitted a charge sheet on October 31, 2024, naming Abul Hossain as the lone accused.
During the trial, the accused gave a confessional statement under Section 164 of the Code of Criminal Procedure before Chuadanga Senior Judicial Magistrate Md Touhidul Islam. DNA tests also confirmed his involvement.
A tribunal sentenced a man to death for killing a child in the port city in 2023 after an attempted rape.
Judge Saifur Rahman of the Chittagong Women and Children Repression Prevention Tribunal-2 delivered the verdict. The convict is identified as Rubel, 35, a vegetable vendor of the Pahartali area.
According to the prosecution, the girl, a class IV student, went missing from on March 21, 2023. A general diary was lodged with Pahartali Police Station the same day, and an abduction case was filed on March 28.
Police arrested Rubel in connection with the case and, following interrogation, recovered the girl's body from a water body on March 29.
Rubel told interrogators he lured her to a house with the promise of a kitten. He then attempted to assault her and, when she raised an alarm, strangled her and dumped her body in a water body in the Alamtara Pukur Par area on the night of March 21.

item.title: Minor girl rape, murder: Security guard placed on 3-day remand
item.description:
A Dhaka court on Monday placed security guard Russell Mridha on a three-day remand for questioning in connection with the rape and murder of a six-year-old girl in capital's West Rampura.
Dhaka Metropolitan Magistrate Hasibuzzaman passed the order after the investigation officer sought his remand. SI Maruf Reza of the prosecution division confirmed the matter.
According to the case statement, the minor girl went missing from West Rampura at around 9:00pm on February 24. Her body was found around 11:30pm, lying face down in water inside the unfinished lift shaft of a nearby under-construction building.
The following day, the girl's father, Liton, filed a case with Hatirjheel Police Station.
Police initially arrested Russell under Section 54 of the Code of Criminal Procedure and sent him to jail. He was later shown arrested in the case and placed on a three-day remand on March 5.
Subsequent forensic examination found evidence that the girl had been raped.
On June 13, the investigation officer SI Jahangir Alam of the Detective Branch's (DB) Mohammadpur Zonal Team, sought a seven-day remand to further question Russell. The court fixed Monday for the hearing in the presence of the accused.
During Monday's hearing, Russell was produced before the court. His lawyer Zahir Hossain sought cancellation of the remand and prayed for bail. After hearing both sides, the court rejected the plea and granted a three-day remand.

ULAB, East West University hold river conservation field practicum
item.description:
The Department of General Education (GED) at the University of Liberal Arts Bangladesh (ULAB), in collaboration with Riverine People, organized a field practicum and sharing session titled "Conservation Learning through the Buriganga–Turag Confluence" on Saturday.
A total of 145 students from ULAB and East West University participated in the program.
The program brought together 105 students from four faculties of East West University and 40 students from ULAB. Accompanied by faculty members from both universities, the participants visited the confluence of the Buriganga and Turag rivers as part of an academic field exercise.
Sheikh Rokon, secretary general of Riverine People, briefed the participants on the historical significance of the Buriganga and Turag rivers. He discussed the major challenges facing the rivers, including encroachment and pollution, and highlighted initiatives undertaken by the government and civil society organizations. He also emphasized the role of students and young people in protecting and restoring the country's rivers.
Following the field visit, a sharing session was held at the ULAB cafeteria.
Professor Shahnaj Husne Jahan, head of the Department of General Education at ULAB, welcomed the participants and expressed appreciation for organizing the field practicum. She emphasized the importance of engaging students in river conservation efforts.
Mumita Tanjeela, associate professor in the Department of Sociology at East West University, appreciated the collaborative initiative and encouraged students to contribute to the conservation of natural resources.
A student representative from ULAB presented the department's activities, focusing on environmental protection and sustainable development initiatives. Srabonti Zaman, a ULAB student, also delivered a presentation on the university's environmental and sustainability initiatives, including river conservation.
F M Anwar Hossain, president of Riverine People, presented an overview of the organization's ongoing activities and research on the Buriganga River. He also outlined future plans and highlighted opportunities for collaboration with academic institutions and other stakeholders.
Among others, the sharing session was attended by Rasel Hussain, assistant professor; Helal Uddin, senior lecturer; and Ruma Parvin, senior lecturer, along with 2 student representatives from East West University. Rajat Kanti Roy, trustee and director (Riverine Archive) of Riverine People, also attended the event.
The program was facilitated by Ahmed Abidur Razzaque Khan, assistant professor in the Department of General Education at ULAB. Mohammed Mamun Or Rashid assisted in coordinating the field visit and managing the event.

item.title: Defense adviser: Cyber capability, local innovation must gain new momentum
item.description:
Bangladesh must strengthen its cyber capability and accelerate local innovation by transforming research into patents, usable technologies and locally developed products to enhance national security and reduce foreign dependence, Prime Minister's Defense Adviser Brigadier General (Retd) Dr AKM Shamsul Islam, PSC, G, said on Monday.
He made the remarks while visiting the Military Institute of Science and Technology (MIST) at Mirpur Cantonment in the capital.
During the visit, the defense adviser was briefed on the institution's modern education system, cybersecurity, research, patent, and technological innovation activities.
He first visited MIST's Cyber Range, where the head of the Computer Science and Engineering Department presented its capabilities, training activities, research opportunities and future plans.
The briefing highlighted the Cyber Range's role in addressing cyber threats, providing training in real-world simulated environments, developing skilled manpower and protecting the country's critical information and digital infrastructure.
The defence adviser said: "In today's world, a country's security is no longer confined to its geographical boundaries. Protecting information, communication systems and critical digital infrastructure is also an essential part of national security."
He emphasized the need to develop skilled cybersecurity professionals while strengthening coordination among educational institutions, government agencies and the domestic technology sector.
Dr AKM Shamsul Islam later met members of the MAVIROV team, who briefed him on their innovation activities, technological capabilities and future plans.
MIST's Research and Development Wing also presented the institution's work on patents and innovation, highlighting technologies developed by its teachers, researchers and students, research-based ideas and practical solutions with real-world applications.
Officials informed the defense adviser that MIST's MATEROV team won the MATEROV International Underwater Robot Competition 2026, held in Newfoundland and Labrador, Canada, from June 23 to 27.
The competition featured 77 teams from 16 countries, with MIST securing the top position.
The defense adviser said: "Research achievements should not remain limited to reports or exhibitions. Effective research must be transformed into patents, usable technologies and locally developed products. This will enhance the country's technological capability, reduce foreign dependence and create new employment opportunities."
He stressed the importance of providing young innovators with research facilities, training, guidance and institutional support.
He also called for expanding research to address practical challenges in public welfare, disaster management, cybersecurity, health, agriculture, and industry.
MIST officials later briefed the defense adviser on the institution's ongoing education and research activities, technological capabilities and future development plans.
He praised the contributions of the institution's teachers, researchers, students and officials to advancing research and innovation.
The visit concluded with a photo session involving the defense adviser, members of the MAVIROV team and senior MIST officials.

item.title: Bangladesh moves toward Sustainable Building Labeling System
item.description:
Bangladesh on Monday took a step toward establishing its national Sustainable Building Labeling System as government officials, United Nations agencies, financial institutions, academics, professional bodies and private sector representatives attended a stakeholder consultation workshop in Dhaka.
The workshop was organized by the Housing and Building Research Institute (HBRI) under the Ministry of Housing and Public Works (MoHPW), in collaboration with the United Nations Human Settlements Program (UN-Habitat), the United Nations Environment Program (UNEP) and the United Nations Office for Project Services (UNOPS), under the Transforming the Built Environment through Sustainable Materials in Bangladesh project funded by the German Federal Ministry for Economic Cooperation and Development (BMZ).
The consultation focused on implementing HBRI-DESH (Design for Environment and Sustainable Habitats) and its digital platform, DESHboard, which will serve as Bangladesh's national labeling system for sustainable, affordable and climate-resilient buildings.
The inaugural session was attended by Most Ferdousi Begum, additional secretary and chairman of the National Housing Authority; Asifur Rahman Bhuiyan, chief architect of the Department of Architecture; and Dr Giasuddin Haider, director general of HBRI. A panel involving representatives from the Ministry of Housing and Public Works, Rajdhani Unnayan Kartripakkha (RAJUK), National Board of Revenue (NBR) and Dhaka Power Distribution Company Limited (DPDC) discussed policy, institutional coordination and financing for implementing the system.
According to the UNEP/GlobalABC Global Status Report 2025-2026, buildings and construction account for nearly 37% of global energy-related carbon dioxide emissions and around half of global material extraction. Bangladesh's urban population has roughly doubled in the past 2 decades and is projected to become majority urban by 2037, with housing demand expected to reach 10.5 million units by 2030, according to the International Finance Corporation (IFC).
Participants included representatives from government agencies, Bangladesh Bank, financial institutions, development partners, universities, professional bodies, real estate developers, the construction industry, environmental experts and civil society organizations.
Most Ferdousi Begum said: "HBRI-DESH is more than a technical framework—it is a nationally owned instrument that will help translate Bangladesh's commitment to sustainable urban development into practical action. Through strong collaboration among government, the private sector and development partners, we can establish a credible and transparent system that promotes sustainable, affordable and climate-resilient buildings across the country."
The consultation produced recommendations on governance, financing, capacity development and digital systems to inform HBRI-DESH's implementation guidelines and phased national rollout. The initiative also supports Bangladesh's Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs) and Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) 11, 12 and 13.

item.title: Three more die of dengue, 372 hospitalized in 24hrs
item.description:
Three people died of dengue, and 372 new patients were admitted to hospitals across the country in the 24 hours to 8am Monday.
With the latest fatalities, the dengue death toll has risen to 37 this year, according to the Directorate General of Health Services (DGHS).
The new deaths were reported in Dhaka South City Corporation, Khulna, and Barisal divisions, it said.
During the reporting period, 347 patients were discharged from hospitals after recovery.
Since January 1, a total of 10,857 dengue patients have been admitted to hospitals across the country. Of them, 9,786 have recovered and been discharged, while 1,034 patients are currently undergoing treatment.

item.title: EX-MP Jakob shown arrested in July uprising attempted murder case
item.description:
A Dhaka court on Monday showed former Bhola-4 lawmaker Abdullah Al Islam Jakob arrested in an attempted murder case filed over the shooting of a protester during the July uprising in Mirpur.
Dhaka Metropolitan Magistrate Ariful Islam passed the order after Sub Inspector Md Ashraful Doula and the investigating officer of Mirpur Model Police Station, sought to have Jakob shown arrested in the case.
Jakob was produced before the court wearing handcuffs, a bulletproof vest and a helmet, according to defence lawyer Md Khair Uddin. In the application, the investigating officer said Awami League activists allegedly attacked anti-discrimination student protesters and opened indiscriminate fire during the movement, leaving complainant Md Rifat Hossain with a bullet wound above his right knee.
The application further stated that Jakob, a former MP for Bhola-4 and an active Awami League member, needed to be shown arrested to ensure a fair investigation.
According to the case statement, Rifat joined the anti-discrimination student movement in front of Mirpur Girls' Ideal School and College in Mirpur Section-10 on the morning of August 4, 2024.
At around 2:00 pm, acting on the accused's instructions, 30 to 40 people, armed with firearms, locally made weapons and sticks, allegedly attacked the protesters and fired indiscriminately, leaving Rifat shot above his right knee.
The complaint also alleged that after he collapsed from the gunshot wound, the attackers kicked, punched and assaulted him with local weapons in an attempt to kill him. Fellow protesters rescued him and took him first to Islami Bank Hospital and later to Kurmitola General Hospital, where he underwent treatment for about a month. Doctors later removed the bullet from his leg through surgery.

item.title: Iran’s interior minister set to visit Islamabad on Monday
item.description:
Iran’s Interior Minister Eskandar Momeni is set to travel to mediator Pakistan’s capital on Monday, a Pakistani interior ministry source said.
The source confirmed to AFP that the minister would have talks with Pakistani officials but did not elaborate on whether the trip was linked to Pakistan’s long-running mediation efforts to end fighting between the United States and Iran.
Iran’s foreign ministry spokesperson had signaled on Monday that diplomatic exchanges with the United States via mediators were ongoing despite US military strikes on the country.
Pakistan said last week it would encourage the warring nations to stop violence and resume talks under a memorandum of understanding it helped mediate last month.
The interim agreement and a possible path towards a permanent halt to the war had come into question when fresh fighting broke out this month.
Momeni has previously taken part in mediation talks and met with Pakistan’s Interior Minister Mohsin Naqvi when he visited Iran.

item.title: Seven PMs since January 2016: Britain’s decade of ‘chaos’
item.description:
Britain on Monday was set to gain its seventh prime minister since the start of 2016 as Andy Burnham takes over following the resignation of Keir Starmer.
Labor had criticized the rapid turnover in leadership of the Conservative Party when it was in office, but then ditched its own unpopular leader within two years of winning the July 2024 elections.
Veteran Labor politician Andy Burnham was to acknowledge this trend in his opening speech, saying he is “acutely conscious” of the need for more stable politics.
Starmer announced his resignation in June following months of nose-diving poll ratings and manoeuvring by his own MPs, who have overwhelmingly backed Burnham to take over.
Starmer had swept to power in a landslide general election victory in July 2024, ending 14 years of Conservative rule.
He had criticized the “chaos” of chopping and changing leaders under the Conservatives, who went through five prime ministers from 2016 to 2024, only to see his party take the same route.
Here are the seven prime ministers in power since the start of 2016:
Britain’s decision to leave the European Union ended Cameron’s second term as prime minister.
After the country voted to leave in a June 2016 referendum, Cameron, who had campaigned to remain in the bloc, resigned.
May took over amid the fallout from the Brexit referendum after a long tenure in the notoriously difficult post of interior minister.
She called a snap election the following year to strengthen her hand in Brexit negotiations, but the move backfired when her party emerged as the biggest in parliament but without a majority.
Unable to get her Brexit deal through parliament, the Conservatives suffered a drubbing in European Parliament elections in May 2019, leading to her resignation.
Johnson, a maverick politician famed for making a career out of breaking the rules, had to navigate the coronavirus pandemic and Britain’s departure from the European Union.
He led the Conservatives to victory in the December 2019 snap general election.
But weakened by scandals he was eventually forced to step down following a cascade of resignations by ministers and aides.
Truss was prime minister for just 49 days, the shortest on record, before being ousted over her disastrous tax-cutting mini-budget.
Her economic agenda spooked the markets and took the UK to the brink of financial meltdown, losing her the support of her own party.
Sunak was at the helm for 20 months before losing the 2024 general election to Starmer, bringing to an end 14 years of Conservative rule.
He brought some stability following the Truss debacle, but failed to stop bitter Tory infighting.
The privately wealthy former financier ultimately failed to connect with regular voters struggling with a cost-of-living crisis.
The 63-year-old won praise internationally for standing up to US President Donald Trump over the Iran war and maintaining European support for Ukraine.
But at home he made unpopular moves to cut welfare, which were watered down by left-wing lawmakers, and to increase business costs amid a cost of living crisis.
He struggled against the rise of the hard-right Reform UK party, led by Nigel Farage and Labor suffered humiliating defeats in local elections in May. And questions were raised over his judgement after he appointed and then sacked Peter Mandelson as US envoy to Washington, over his ties to late Us sex offender Jeffrey Epstein.

item.title: Germany’s 5,000 heat deaths: What the numbers reveal
item.description:
A heatstroke begins with headaches, dizziness and loss of awareness. The body’s regulatory system fails, causing body temperature to rise to life-threatening levels. Multiple organ failure and death can follow.
But doctors rarely record deaths that can be directly attributed to heat. According to Germany’s Federal Statistical Office, an average of 21 such cases were recorded each year between 2004 and 2014.
The 5,120 heat-related deaths in Germany in 2026 through June 28, published by the Robert Koch Institute (RKI), is therefore an estimate. But that doesn’t mean the figure is pulled out of thin air.
“It’s based on a statistical correlation,” said Alexandra Schneider, a meteorologist, epidemiologist and deputy director of the Institute of Epidemiology at Helmholtz Munich.
This estimate was found by comparing death figures collected by Germany’s Federal Statistical Office with temperature trends over a given period measured by the German Weather Service.
In the final week of June alone, around 23,600 people died. The weekly average temperature — meaning the average daytime and nighttime temperature over the course of a week — was 26 degrees Celsius (79 Fahrenheit). The RKI assumes that heat-related deaths occur from a weekly average temperature of 20 degrees Celsius onward.
The number of deaths in that final week of June was nearly 30% higher than the average for comparable periods in previous years, when around 18,200 people died. To estimate the number of heat-related deaths, researchers modeled how many deaths would have occurred under conditions with temperatures of no more than 20 degrees Celsius.
Researchers also adjust for certain confounding factors, Schneider explained. Using this method, the RKI estimates that there were 5,120 heat-related deaths — with 4,310 of those occurring in the final week of June.
Schneider considers the estimate plausible and said she expected a figure of this magnitude. In the past, the epidemiologist has criticized the RKI’s methodology.
“If temperatures fluctuate sharply within a week, using a weekly average can smooth out those extremes and lead to an underestimation of deaths,” she explained. “This time, however, it was consistently hot.”
The same applies to cold-related deaths as to heat-related deaths: they are estimates based on a plausible statistical link. During colder months, respiratory illnesses become more common. Cold temperatures also contribute to cardiovascular diseases, just as heat does, said Schneider.
“In Europe, cold-associated mortality is still far higher than heat-associated mortality,” she said. “But we are seeing a slow shift.” Could climate change perhaps lead to milder winters and therefore fewer deaths? Researchers have also looked into this question and modeled different scenarios.
Regardless of how favorable the scenarios are, Schneider said, they all point to the same conclusion: “the net effect — meaning the total number of deaths — increases.” This is because the rise in deaths attributed to heat is so significant that it can’t be offset by the decline in cold-associated deaths.
Schneider said focusing solely on heatstroke as a cause of death linked to high temperatures would massively underplay the impact of heat. “That is why these statistical methods are used to identify and examine links between heat and other chronic diseases,” she said.
She herself has contributed to studies showing a connection between heat and certain illnesses. “We were able to show that heat is now strongly associated with heart attacks,” Schneider said. Nighttime heat also increases the risk of strokes.
Jonas Sonnenstuhl, a paramedic in Teltow, Brandenburg, has firsthand experience with heat-related deaths. “We all know that conditions like strokes and heart attacks become more frequent and can also become life-threatening more quickly,” he said.
Schneider said heat-related deaths are only the tip of the iceberg. Even when high temperatures don’t lead to death, they place a strain on people’s health — especially those with preexisting conditions.
During the June heat wave, Sonnenstuhl treated a 17-year-old patient with a congenital heart defect who had called emergency services. “She showed clear symptoms that day, which could already be traced back to the fact that her body was at its limit,” he said. Shortness of breath, dizziness and impaired consciousness.
The heat also pushed emergency responders and hospital staff to their physical limits. On June 28, when Sonnenstuhl was working a 24-hour shift, the temperature inside the ambulance did not fall below 30 degrees Celsius. Heavy protective clothing, steel-toe boots and physically demanding work added to the strain.
Many hospital emergency departments are not air-conditioned, and emergency stations are even less likely to be. “Both we and the hospital staff were at our limit,” said Sonnenstuhl. For people whose job it is to save lives, he added, staying cool in an emergency is essential.

item.title: Why Gulf states are investing billions in Africa
item.description:
The news came out just a few days ago: Adnoc Distribution, a petroleum retailer in Abu Dhabi, the capital of the United Arab Emirates, will take over energy giant Shell’s petrol stations and fuel business in South Africa. The price is thought to be around $1 billion (€870 million). With this purchase Abu Dhabi has assured itself of a place in the African continent’s fuel market.
This billion-dollar deal is just one example of a larger trend. Even taking into account the occasional small declines, the Gulf states have been growing their economic presence in Africa for years now.
According to the UK-based think tank Chatham House, the countries belonging to the Gulf Cooperation Council or GCC, have invested more than $100 billion in Africa over the past decade. Around $59 billion of that came from the UAE and another $26 billion from Saudi Arabia.
“For the Gulf states, Africa isn’t some far away region it’s right in their neighborhood,” said Stephan Roll, a senior fellow in the Africa and Middle East division at the German Institute for International and Security Affairs. In eastern Africa, there are central trade routes and the two regions have had social and economic ties for decades. Nobody should be surprised by the Gulf states’ increasing engagement in Africa, he told DW.
“One of the biggest reasons why they started to look at Africa differently, especially over the past 10 years or so, has been the need to diversify away from hydrocarbons and also strengthen their own economic projection,” Maddalena Procopio, a senior policy fellow in the Africa program at the European Council on Foreign Relations, explained. “But they started to also see the rest of Africa as an incredible potential market, where they could basically have revenues in sectors that they haven’t ventured into.”
According to analyses by a number of institutions, including the Brookings Institution, Chatham House and the African Development Bank Group, investment from out of the Gulf is focused on energy, ports, logistics, agriculture and critical raw materials. These investments make sense as they secure trade routes, strengthen food security and ensure access to raw materials like
cooper, cobalt and lithium, which are needed for the development of electric vehicles and artificial intelligence.
The countries involved are pursuing different strategies though. Saudi Arabia and the UAE are mainly investing in renewable energies and in the processing and distribution of petroleum products, Procopio noted. Qatar, which has played a less significant role in Africa up until now, is more focused on selected economic cooperation, she said.
The UAE is seen by experts as the country most involved. “You need to see their policies as a whole package,” Roll pointed out. Ports, logistics and economic interests cannot be separated from their foreign policy and security goals, he said.
Having control over strategically located ports doesn’t just offer the UAE an economic advantage, it also allows them political influence over important global trade routes. Procopio confirmed that the UAE’s economic engagement with African nations was larger than that of other Gulf states because of its foreign policy and security interests.
Saudi Arabia is a lot more selective, Roll added, and is concentrating on certain sectors, particularly energy. The Saudis also played an important role in development financing on a bilateral basis as well as through multilateral institutions such as the Islamic Development Bank, he said. And he did not think the Saudis were interested in competing with the UAE when it came to investment.
Procopio believed that the disparities in investment strategies could be explained by some foundational differences in the economies of those two countries.
The UAE’s “development model has been based abroad because the country is very small,” she said. “So it needs to trade with the rest of the world to grow. And if it wants to diversify away from oil and gas, it needs to build many commercial relationships abroad, many more than Saudi Arabia.”
Saudi Arabia, a much larger country, needed to align foreign trade goals more closely with its own plans for economic transformation, she said.
The UAE’s commercial footprint also has a “strong political dimension … with investments aiming to project power and to challenge Saudi Arabia’s regional standing,” Procopio argued.
For a lot of African nations, interest from out of the Gulf comes at a good time. According to estimates by the African Development Bank the continent’s financial needs are growing even as Western development funding shrinks and China reduces the amount of loans on offer. Direct investment from the Gulf states could help to plug gaps in the financing of things such as infrastructure, energy and logistics.
Procopio also saw another advantage. Unlike China, the Gulf states preferred investment to loans, she said. So in comparison, Gulf financing was often available more easily and didn’t come with as many political conditions. And African states were not bound to just one other country; they still had the opportunity to broaden their international alliances.
The Gulf states’ financial engagement in Africa doesn’t come without controversy. Chatham House has warned that the investments are concentrated on ports, supply chains and raw materials, which mostly serves the strategic interests of the financing states. The Brookings Institution has cautioned that some of the investments reduced the role of African states simply to suppliers of raw materials. What would be more helpful, it has said, would be to aid manufacturing and industry instead.
Roll also saw this as a challenge. The investments themselves were not problematic; it was the new dependencies they created through strategic infrastructure or the export of unprocessed raw materials. Economic interests and geopolitical policy were not necessarily separate, he argued.
Procopio was more cautious. Partnerships between African countries and the Gulf states were really at an early stage, she pointed out. Whether the billions coming out of the UAE, Saudi Arabia or Qatar would eventually lead to more industrialization remained to be seen.
That is where the real challenge lies. The source of the investment alone won’t decide how successful any financial partnership is. It will depend on how African nations build value from the money. That is what the Brookings Institution and the African Development Bank both point to: Foreign direct investment can be an important driver of economic development but it will only be sustainable if it supports African domestic industry and long-term growth.

item.title: Guyana: Dozens feared dead after ferry capsizes
item.description:
Several people were feared dead after a ferry carrying more than 100 people capsized off the coast of Guyana, authorities said.
The ferry MV Barima left Guyana at the northeast corner of South America on Saturday afternoon, carrying at least 133 people for a trip from the capital Georgetown to Port Kaituma.
Public Works Minister Juan Edghill said that the list of people on board was not “a true reflection of the occupants on board,” asking family members and loved ones to reach out to officials to create an official tally of those missing.
The inaccuracy in the list of passengers has created an additional challenge as authorities do not know how many people they are searching for.
The rescue operations were launched after air traffic control received a distress call from the ferry around 11pm local time on Saturday.
Rescue teams have so far saved 67 people. The rescued victims include 41 men, 11 women, six boys and nine girls, Prime Minister Mark Phillips said in a statement Sunday.
“The families of those rescued, as well as the relatives of all recorded passengers and crew members, have been engaged,” Phillips said, adding that “the captain and mate of the vessel, who were among those rescued, have returned to the area to assist with the search and rescue operation.”
However, no survivors have been found since Sunday afternoon.
“Search and rescue is still in progress,” Public Works Minister ‌Edghill said. “So to the families that are out there wondering about their loved ones, we are doing everything that is humanly possible to find them.”
Prime Minister Mark Phillips said investigators are looking into the incident, adding that two crew members were found to have tested positive for cannabis.
Phillips said that the vessel, which had had maintenance in 2024 and was due for an inspection later this year, reported a cargo weight of 268 tons out of a 284-ton-capacity.
It was licensed to carry more than 300 passengers and it was equipped with 250 life jackets, six inflatable life rafts and two rigid life rafts, according to the prime minister.
“Let me be absolutely clear: where negligence, misconduct or criminal wrongdoing is established, those responsible will face the full force of the law,” Phillips said in a Sunday evening press conference.

BSTI orders withdrawing 8 products from market
item.description:
The Bangladesh Standards and Testing Institution (BSTI) has ordered the withdrawal of eight product variants belonging to four companies from the market, as they failed to meet the benchmarks set by the Bangladesh Standards (BDS) during laboratory testing.
In a press release issued on Monday, BSTI stated that the tests revealed excessive preservatives, the presence of microorganisms harmful to human health, and deviations from the prescribed standards in these products.
Consequently, the respective companies have been ordered to withdraw these items from the market.
According to BSTI, the mango pickle of Fresh Garden Agro Resources was found to contain preservatives exceeding the permissible limit.
Additionally, the Rotondo hazelnut coated chocolate ice cream was found to contain total milk solids non-fat higher than the level set by the Bangladesh Standards.
On the other hand, harmful microorganisms were detected in several products of Olive Bangladesh Limited, including its Labanya brand milk skin lotion, alvera skin lotion, olive and Labanya brand Facewash Moringa+Matcha, foaming milk & tulsi, and Labanya brand alvera shampoo.
Furthermore, the Mango and Orange variants of Soft Drink Powder under the Dekko Fruit Funda brand of Dekko Foods Limited were found to contain less Vitamin C than the required levels stipulated by Bangladesh standards.
BSTI stated that rejection letters have already been issued to the concerned companies regarding these failed products.
At the same time, directions have been given to suspend the production, sale, and distribution of these specific items until their licenses are renewed or re-approved.
BSTI will closely monitor whether this directive is being properly implemented.
The agency warned that strict legal action will be taken against the non-compliant companies under existing laws if the directive is violated.
BSTI added that ensuring product quality in line with national standards is the legal and moral responsibility of every licensed institution.
The agency remains determined to take necessary administrative and legal measures to safeguard public interest and consumer rights if companies fail to uphold this responsibility.
BSTI director general (secretary) Kazi Imdadul Hoque stated that BSTI is committed to ensuring that the public can purchase quality products.
He also urged consumers to promptly report to the agency any complaints regarding product quality, adulteration, fraud, or the misuse of the BSTI certification mark.

item.title: Brac University welcomes three new members to its board of trustees
item.description:
Brac University welcomed three distinguished new members to its board of trustees, strengthening the board’s collective expertise in higher education, academic leadership, financial governance and institutional stewardship at an important stage in the university’s growth.
The new trustees are Claire Gordon, director of the Eden Centre for Education Enhancement at the London School of Economics and Political Science (LSE); Masud Khan, chairman of the Bangladesh Securities and Exchange Commission and former chairman of Unilever Consumer Care Ltd, and Prof Imran Rahman, former vice-chancellor of the University of Liberal Arts Bangladesh.
Their appointments were approved in accordance with Brac University’s governance process and reflect the university’s commitment to drawing on national and international expertise as it advances its next phase of institutional development.
“At this important moment in Brac University’s journey, we are pleased to welcome three highly accomplished individuals to the Board of Trustees,” said Tamara Hasan Abed, chairperson of the Brac University Board of Trustees.
“Their experience in global higher education, university leadership, financial governance and institutional development will bring valuable perspectives as Brac University continues to strengthen teaching and learning, student experience and research for impact,” she said.
Claire Gordon brings extensive experience in educational development, curriculum enhancement, academic promotion and career development, digital innovation and inclusive practice in higher education.
Her work at LSE includes leading on teaching and learning innovation and the institutional approach to generative AI in education, alongside fostering international academic collaboration.
Her appointment will add a global perspective to Brac University's continuing efforts to strengthen curriculum, pedagogy and educational quality.
Masud Khan brings decades of experience in financial management, corporate governance, capital markets, audit oversight and board leadership across multinational and national institutions.
His expertise will support the board’s fiduciary responsibilities and contribute to the university’s long-term financial sustainability, governance resilience and institutional accountability.
Prof Imran Rahman brings deep experience in university leadership, business education, academic governance and institutional development.
A former vice-chancellor of Ulab and a long-serving academic leader, he has played an important role in advancing higher education practice in Bangladesh.
His experience will support Brac University’s continued focus on student-centered education, academic quality and institutional excellence.
Brac University said the appointments come at a significant point in its journey as the university continues to build on its founding commitment to academic excellence, social impact and inclusive development.
The addition of the new trustees will further strengthen the board’s role in guiding the university’s strategic priorities and long-term institutional growth.

item.title: How new changes to savings certificates will impact tax returns
item.description:
National Savings Certificates (Sanchayapatra) are no longer a shelter for tax insulation.
Under the new guidelines for the FY2026-27 assessment year, the National Board of Revenue (NBR) has fundamentally decoupled how interest income from these instruments is taxed, introducing a major structural shift that eliminates the long-standing "final tax liability" status.
For the first time, source tax deducted from savings certificate profits will be treated strictly as an advance tax.
This shift means that while over-taxed, small-scale savers can now claim direct tax refunds, high-income earners face a significantly elevated tax burden due to a simultaneous slashing of tax investment rebates.
Key highlights of the new tax framework
The transition from a final withholding tax to an adjustable advance tax fundamentally shifts the compliance burden onto the taxpayer.
If a regular investor has Tk30,000 deducted at source throughout the fiscal year, but final calculations put their true tax liability at Tk20,000, the NBR will refund the excess Tk10,000.
To claim this refund, taxpayers must explicitly request it in their annual return by providing a verified bank account number.
Crucially, the National Savings Directorate has begun distributing the mandatory Source Tax Deduction Certificate across district and bureau offices, which serves as the ultimate proof of deduction required during filing.
Winners and losers
While the government has kept nominal savings certificate interest rates unchanged—ranging between 11.77% and 11.98% to protect middle-class dependencies—the underlying fiscal changes create distinct groups of winners and losers.
Small savers, marginal income earners, and retirees whose annual total income sits below the tax-free threshold stand to benefit the most.
Previously, the 5% or 10% tax sliced from their yields was permanently retained by the state.
They can now fully recover these deductions, provided they hold a Taxpayer Identification Number (TIN) and submit a return.
Conversely, affluent investors face an aggressive squeeze.
Previously, a taxpayer in the highest 25% or 30% bracket only paid a flat 10% final tax at source on their savings certificates.
Now, that interest income is pooled directly into their primary income stream, exposing those profits to their highest applicable tax bracket and forcing a major end-of-year tax settlement.
Compounding the impact on high earners is a sharp contraction in investment tax relief.
Although eligible sectors like Life Insurance and Deposit Pension Schemes (DPS)—which retains its Tk1.2 lakh annual deduction cap—remain unchanged, the financial utility of these tax shelters has been minimized:
Rebate Parameter
Former Rules
New FY27 Rules
Rebate Percentage
15% of total allowable investment
10% of total allowable investment
Max Investment Limit
Tk10,000,000
Tk7,500,000
This double-edged policy ensures that while the state honors refunds for lower-income tiers, it simultaneously claws back substantial revenue from upper-income earners who previously used savings certificates to legally minimize their tax liabilities.
Digital transition
The NBR is leveraging these policy adjustments to fast-track its broader digital tax agenda.
Following last year's mandatory online filing pilot for select classes, the e-return system is expanding to encompass all individual filers, with individual portals expected to go live by the final week of July.
Concurrently, the bureau is preparing to pilot its first automated corporate return portal later this year.
To smooth the filing curve, the NBR is utilizing a carrots-and-sticks timeline based on submission dates.
[NBR Return Submission & Penalty Timeline]
Jul 1 – Sep 30 ──▶ 5% Tax Incentive (Up to Tk25,000 Reward)
Oct 1 – Dec 31 ──▶ Standard Filing Windows (No Incentives / No Penalties)
Jan 1 – Mar 31 ──▶ 2% Penalty Applied (Capped at Tk3,000)
Apr 1 – Jun 30 ──▶ 5% Penalty Applied (Capped at Tk5,000 / New Taxpayer Deadline)

item.title: Electro Mart attains 2 recognitions at SDG Brand Champion Awards 2026
item.description:
Electro Mart Group, that manufactures Konka, Gree and Haiko electronics products in Bangladesh, won two recognitions at the SDG Brand Champion Awards 2026.
Fifty-nine sustainable brand initiatives were recognized for the contributions to advancing the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals in Bangladesh at the "SDG Brand Champion Awards 2026", held at the Radisson Blu Water Garden Hotel in Dhaka recently.
Electro Mart’s first award was special contribution in achieving UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs).
This award is given for special contribution to United Nations SDGs through manufacturing, marketing and after sales services of electronics products in Bangladesh.
Electro Mart Group has been conducting every activity in Bangladesh to fight poverty, inequality, employment, climate and environmental protection and achieve the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals.
Electro Mart also implants sustainability across its production and product life cycle by adopting green technologies, renewable energy, responsible materials, and eco-friendly design principles, aligning its manufacturing processes with multiple SDGs, notably SDG- 7, 8, 9, 12, and 13.
Electro Mart Limited aligns its initiatives with five United Nations Sustainable Development Goals.
Their manufacturing plant has generated over 7,000 direct and 10,000 indirect jobs while increasing contributions to government revenue through duties, value-added tax (VAT), and corporate taxes.
The company stated that its local manufacturing model, supported by technology transfers from China's Gree and Konka brands, aims to lower local electricity usage and reduce environmental waste through the production of energy-efficient home appliances.
Konka and Gree brand electronics & home appliances products started its journey in Bangladesh through Electro Mart Group.
Through technology sharing with China, the group is now manufacturing and marketing eco-friendly and latest technology enriched electronics and home appliance products to the Bangladeshi customer.
Konka, Gree and Haiko brands of electronics and home appliance products are now the first choice of consumers worldwide due to their unique features, quality, acceptability, after-sales services and affordable prices.
Konka has several home appliance products including refrigerators, freezers, LED TVs, washing machines, microwave ovens, mixer grinders, electric kettles, gas stoves, infrared cookers, pressure cookers, rice cookers, electric irons.
Gree has ceiling, wall, cassette, VRF, ducted & chiller air conditioners, portable ACs, air purifiers, dehumidifiers, air coolers, air circulator fans and air curtains.
As a result of endless love, trust and reliance of customers, well-wishers and consumers in the past, Electro Mart Group is now gaining recognition country and worldwide as well.
Group DMD Nurul Afser said: “Every recognition creates new responsibility which will make us more responsible and motivated to work in every moment of the future. We express our deep gratitude to customers, well-wishers, buyers and consumers for their unwavering trust, endless love and confidence in our products and services.”

item.title: Premier Bank, Omnicare holds health awareness session for female employees
item.description:
The Premier Bank PLC, in collaboration with Omnicare, organized a special health awareness session for its female employees at the Learning & Talent Development Centre of the bank’s head office.
The initiative was designed to emphasize women’s physical and mental wellbeing and recognize their valuable contributions in the workplace.
The session was attended by Md Monzur Mofiz, managing director of Premier Bank PLC; Niamat Uddin Ahmed, deputy managing director; Amalendu Roy, deputy managing director; and Moinul Huq Shiraze, VP and head of human resources (in-charge).
The program was moderated by Syeda Noureen Binte Naquib, SAVP of the bank’s Learning & Talent Development Centre.
In his address, managing director Md Monzur Mofiz highlighted the significant role women play across families, communities and society as a whole.
He stated that ensuring the wellbeing, safety and empowerment of women is fundamental to fostering an inclusive, supportive, and positive workplace culture.
The session featured expert insights from Dr Refat Uz Johra, assistant professor and psychiatrist at Omnicare, who discussed mental wellbeing, stress management, and maintaining a healthy work-life balance.
Dr Sain Faria Setu, specialist in obstetrics and gynecology at Omnicare, shared valuable information on women’s reproductive health, the importance of regular health check-ups and adopting a health-conscious lifestyle.
Female employees of Premier Bank actively participated in the interactive session, which provided an engaging platform for learning, awareness and open discussion on key health and wellness issues affecting women.

item.title: Akij Insaf Group unveils The Grandeur & Playfinity
item.description:
Akij Insaf Group announced the opening of The Grandeur and Playfinity, two landmark lifestyle destinations that redefine how people dine, play, and spend quality time together.
These new ventures promise to set a new benchmark for premium leisure experiences in Bangladesh.
The organizations were formally inaugurated by Sk Jamil Uddin, managing director of Akij Insaf Group, marking the official launch of The Grandeur and Playfinity.
Located under one roof, The Grandeur is a premium food destination where diverse culinary traditions come together to create memorable dining experiences.
Carefully curated with a modern ambience, elegant interiors, and an exceptional selection of local and international cuisines, The Grandeur has been designed as the perfect destination for families, friends, professionals, and food enthusiasts seeking quality, comfort, and unforgettable experiences.
Complementing this experience is Playfinity, an immersive next-generation entertainment destination that brings together unique gaming, interactive attractions, and exciting recreational experiences for visitors of all ages.
Inspired by futuristic space exploration, Playfinity offers an engaging environment where imagination, adventure, and technology come together, making every visit a memorable journey.
The launch reflects Akij Insaf Group's continued investment in innovation, hospitality, and customer experience.
By combining premium dining with immersive entertainment, the Group aims to introduce a destination where every visit becomes an occasion worth celebrating.
Visitors can look forward to a vibrant atmosphere, carefully designed spaces, signature culinary offerings, exciting gaming experiences, and memorable moments that appeal to guests of all generations.
With the inauguration of The Grandeur and Playfinity, Akij Insaf Group embarks on a new chapter in Bangladesh's lifestyle and entertainment landscape—one that celebrates togetherness, innovation, and unforgettable experiences.

item.title: HSBC wins 2 awards at Euromoney Awards for Excellence 2026
item.description:
HSBC has been recognized with two prestigious accolades in the Euromoney Awards for Excellence 2026 for its performance in Bangladesh, being named the Best Bank for Sustainable Finance and the Best Bank for Large Corporates in Bangladesh.
The awards reflect the bank’s unwavering commitment to customer-centricity, technological innovation, and operational excellence.
Md Mahbub ur Rahman, chief executive officer, HSBC Bangladesh, stated: “We’re proud to be recognised once again for the strength of our large corporate banking and our leadership in sustainable finance. This reflects the depth of our capabilities, the commitment of our teams, and our focus on helping clients grow, transform, and deliver long-term value for building a sustainable future in Bangladesh.”
Operating in Bangladesh for almost 30 years, HSBC has been partnering in the nation’s economic progress - supporting trade, investment, infrastructure and innovation across industries.
The bank is committed to building on its strong platform for growth for its customers in Bangladesh.
HSBC’s group chief executive Georges Elhedery was awarded Global Banker of the Year, while across Asia, HSBC won some of Euromoney’s most competitive categories, including Best Bank in Asia.
These awards reflect the operational excellence HSBC delivered within its new simple and agile structure.
In Bangladesh, HSBC stays close to its customers, takes ownership, and delivers with pace. Among many consistent successes throughout the year, HSBC earned awards at every level – locally, regionally, and globally.
The Euromoney Awards for Excellence are widely regarded as the industry benchmark, celebrating institutions that demonstrate exceptional leadership in delivering comprehensive, high-quality services to their clients.

item.title: AUW launches Chevron-sponsored Math and Science Summer School 2026
item.description:
The Asian University for Women (AUW), with support from Chevron Bangladesh, on Monday inaugurated the Math and Science Summer School 2026 at its campus in Chattogram.
The four-week program brings together 104 female students from across Bangladesh for an immersive learning experience designed to inspire interest and build capabilities in Science, Technology, Engineering, and Mathematics (Stem).
Since 2019, Chevron Bangladesh has partnered with AUW to expand access to quality Stem education for young women and till 2025, already over 380 young women successfully graduated from this program.
The AUW Math and Science Summer School is designed to strengthen students’ academic skills, critical thinking, and confidence through engaging, hands-on learning experiences.
The program seeks to equip participants with the knowledge and motivation needed to pursue higher education and careers in science and technology.
This year’s cohort of 104 students has been selected from across Bangladesh to participate in a comprehensive curriculum covering mathematics, physics, chemistry, computer coding, and public health.
The courses will be delivered by distinguished international and local instructors affiliated with institutions including the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT), the University of Oxford, American University in Dubai, the University of Chattogram, and other leading academic institutions.
The inauguration ceremony was attended by Eric M Walker, president and managing director of Chevron Bangladesh, as chief guest.
Addressing the students, he highlighted the transformative role of STEM education in fostering innovation, supporting national development, and creating opportunities for future generations of women leaders.
“The future of Stem is not somewhere else. It is right here, in this room” said Eric M Walker, president and managing director of Chevron Bangladesh.
"When young women are encouraged to dream boldly and pursue their interests in science and technology, there is no limit to what they can accomplish.”
Muhammad Imrul Kabir, director of corporate affairs, Chevron Bangladesh, attended the event as special guest.
Other Chevron Bangladesh representatives present included AKM Arif Akhter, head of community engagement & sustainability; Ali Ashraf Choudhury, adviser, community engagement & sustainability; MA Raquib, field corporate affairs adviser, and Md Rashedul Alam, Dhaka security coordinator.
Representing AUW were Sangita Rayamajhi, interim vice chancellor; Haseena Khan, director, Antimicrobial Research Centre, and Mohiuddin Ahsanul Kabir Chowdhury, director, AUW Math and Science Summer School, who welcomed the students and guests.
The inauguration ceremony was coordinated by Fatema Mehejabin; Sharna Biswas Eity, and Abeda Sultana.
Chevron Bangladesh and AUW share a common vision of expanding opportunities for women through education and leadership development.
Over the years, Chevron’s support has helped strengthen and expand the impact of the Summer School program, enabling more young women to access high-quality Stem learning experiences.

Stakeholders call for sustainable financing to prevent child drowning
item.description:
Speakers at a stakeholder dialogue on Sunday called for sustainable financing and stronger multi-stakeholder collaboration to strengthen child drowning prevention efforts in Bangladesh.
They stressed that, alongside government funding, greater participation from the private sector, local government institutions, development partners, the media and other stakeholders is essential to ensure the long-term sustainability of drowning prevention initiatives.
The dialogue, titled "Prevention of Child Drowning and Sustainable Financing for Child Protection," was held at The Daily Star Centre in Dhaka. It was organised by Somashte Media Communication and Development Foundation, with support from the Global Health Advocacy Incubator (GHAI).
The event brought together government officials, representatives from the private sector, development partners, media professionals and public health experts.
Delivering the welcome address, Mir Masruruzzaman, executive director of Somashte, said an average of 40 children die from drowning every day in Bangladesh, yet the issue receives inadequate attention.
He said most child drowning incidents occur between 9am and 2pm, when caregivers are often occupied with work, leaving children without adequate supervision.
"A child can drown not only in large water bodies but also in shallow water. Ensuring safe supervision is therefore critical to preventing drowning," he said.
Rezaul Haque, Somashte's director of research and communications, presented an overview of drowning deaths in Bangladesh, the national strategy, the current financing framework and the potential role of stakeholders, including the private sector.
He said the national action plan aims to reduce drowning deaths by 50% by 2030. More than 18,000 people currently die from drowning each year. However, he said clearer funding sources, dedicated budget lines and expenditure-tracking mechanisms are needed to implement the plan effectively.
The presentation highlighted the Integrated Community-Based Childcare and SwimSafe (ICBC) project, which has provided childcare services, swimming lessons, CPR training, safe rescue education, water safety measures and awareness activities in 16 districts. The project's second phase is currently awaiting approval.
Professor Dr Kazi Maruful Islam of the Department of Development Studies at the University of Dhaka said drowning prevention should be viewed as a continuous social service rather than a project. He emphasised integrating such initiatives into existing local government structures.
Jamira Shabnam, deputy secretary of the Finance Division, called for coordinated initiatives prioritising high-risk areas. She said the central government, local government institutions and other stakeholders all have important roles to play in preventing drowning.
Reaz Ahmad, editor of Dhaka Tribune, said existing projects should be expanded rather than discontinued. He stressed the need for risk-based planning and data-driven management of drowning prevention efforts.
Azharul Haque Azad, managing director of Sadakalo, said raising public awareness about drowning is crucial. He added that collaboration between the media and business organisations could help build large-scale social initiatives.
Zahidul Islam Sajal, head of public communications at Nagad, said the social importance and measurable impact of drowning prevention must be clearly communicated to encourage greater private sector participation.
"Corporate organisations need to clearly understand both the importance of the issue and the opportunities for engagement," he said, adding that digital platforms also offer significant potential for public engagement and fundraising.
Sohrab Hasan, editor of Charcha.com, stressed the need to strengthen public awareness and community initiatives, particularly at the local level.
Media representatives, including Kajal Ghosh of Manab Zamin, Rabeya Baby of The Daily Ittefaq, Partha Shankar Saha of Prothom Alo, Zahid Rahman of Channel i and Saiduzzaman Rowshan of Agami Shomoy, discussed the role of the media in raising awareness and promoting policy attention to drowning prevention.
Sazia Afrin, communications manager at Synergos, said community acceptance of the ICBC project has been strong.
"The challenge now is ensuring these activities continue not only during the project period but also after it ends," she said.
Speakers concluded that while government funding should remain the primary source of financing, long-term sustainability will require complementary support from the private sector, corporate social responsibility initiatives, development partners and local stakeholders.
They also called for dedicated budget allocations across relevant ministries, stronger local government involvement, greater private sector participation and a transparent reporting mechanism to support effective implementation of drowning prevention initiatives.

item.title: What can we learn from the measles outbreak?
Dhaka sees moderate air quality on Monday morning
item.description:
Dhaka, the capital of Bangladesh, witnessed moderate air quality on Monday morning, ranking 17th among cities with the worst air pollution worldwide.
At 9:45am, the capital recorded an Air Quality Index (AQI) score of 90, indicating moderate air quality, according to the air quality monitoring platform IQAir.
A moderate AQI indicates that air quality is generally acceptable. However, people who are unusually sensitive to air pollution may experience minor to moderate health effects if exposed for prolonged periods.
Kampala, Uganda, topped the list of the world's most polluted cities with an AQI score of 159, followed by Kinshasa, Democratic Republic of the Congo, at 158.
Both cities recorded air quality classified as "unhealthy."
The AQI is calculated based on the concentration of major air pollutants, including particulate matter (PM2.5 and PM10), nitrogen dioxide (NO₂), carbon monoxide (CO), sulphur dioxide (SO₂) and ozone (O₃).
According to the AQI scale, a score between 51 and 100 is considered moderate, while 101–150 is deemed unhealthy for sensitive groups, 151–200 is unhealthy, 201–300 is very unhealthy, and 301 or above is classified as hazardous, posing serious health risks to the entire population.
Dhaka has long struggled with air pollution, with air quality typically deteriorating during the dry winter months and improving during the monsoon season due to rainfall and stronger winds.
The World Health Organization estimates that air pollution causes around seven million deaths globally each year, mainly due to stroke, heart disease, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, lung cancer and acute respiratory infections.

item.title: Three new Sylhet wells to add 3 crore cubic feet of gas daily
item.description:
State-owned Sylhet Gas Fields Limited (SGFL) has taken an initiative to drill three new gas and oil wells as part of efforts to ease the country's gas shortage and reduce dependence on costly imports of liquefied natural gas (LNG).
The wells – Rashidpur-13, Dupitila-1 and Kailashtila-9 – are expected to add 3 crore cubic feet of gas per day to the national grid once drilling is completed, officials said.
At present, SGFL supplies around 14.20 crore cubic feet of gas daily to the national grid from its 17 functioning wells.
SGFL Managing Director Engr Md Faruque Hossain said: “SGFL is currently supplying 14.2 crore cubic feet of gas to the national grid every day. Once drilling of the three wells is completed, an additional 3 crore cubic feet of gas can be added to the grid."
Energy experts stressed the need to intensify domestic gas and oil exploration instead of relying heavily on LNG imports, which require substantial foreign currency expenditure each year.
They said Bangladesh has significant untapped hydrocarbon potential both onshore and offshore and called for expanding exploration activities through faster coordination with local and international companies.
Prof Dr Muhammad Farhad Howladar of the Department of Petroleum and Mining Engineering at Shahjalal University of Science and Technology said accelerating exploration activities is essential to ensuring the country's long-term energy security.
Established under Petrobangla, SGFL discovered Bangladesh's first natural gas field in 1955.
Apart from natural gas, the company's production currently meets around 30-35% of the country's petrol demand, 12-15% of octane demand, about 9% of diesel demand and nearly 2% of kerosene demand.
